subject: [PATCH] generic/747: handle ENOSPC gracefully during write/delete cycles

reviews.author: Leo Martins (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged that the test's failure rate is high and agreed to restructure in v2, but did not provide a specific plan for addressing the issue.
reviews.raw_body:
On Tue, 24 Mar 2026 16:33:26 -0700 Leo Martins <loemra.dev@gmail.com> wrote:

Forgot to CC fstests mailing list.

reviews.author: Christoph Hellwig
reviews.summary: Christoph Hellwig noted that the patch's ENOSPC handling may cause unexpected issues due to btrfs' statfs implementation overestimating available space, and suggested addressing this underlying filesystem bug for the benefit of users.
reviews.raw_body:
f_bavail overestimating is a btrfs implementation bug that needs fixing.
It can be wrong, but it needs to be too low in doubt.  Without this
you're going to cause unexpected ENOSPC for real life applictions as
well (and btrfs does have a bit of a reputation for that, so fixing
this properly will benefit your users!)

2026-03-24.patch_summary: This patch addresses a test failure issue in the generic/747 test, which consistently fails on btrfs due to ENOSPC errors during write/delete cycles. The problem arises from statfs overestimating available space at high fill levels, causing dd to hit ENOSPC even when there's room. To handle this, the patch breaks out of the fill loop when full, deletes a file to free space and retry on failure, and redirects dd stderr to preserve errors for debugging purposes. The test is designed to stress garbage collection through write/delete churn, not to validate space accounting.

reviews.author: Qu Wenruo
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Qu Wenruo suggested that the issue of ENOSPC during write/delete cycles could be addressed by improving the calculation of f_bavail, rather than handling it in the test code.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo suggested that the issue in patch generic/747 is caused by overestimation of available space due to two factors: btrfs_calc_available_data_space() not handling uneven disk spaces and total_free_data calculation not considering reserved space. He recommended trying a fix for this problem.
reviews.raw_body:
If this is the problem, then I believe we should fix our f_bavail 
calculation.

---

From what I can see, there are several different ways that f_bavail is 
overestimated:

- btrfs_calc_available_data_space()
   That functions goes through each device and try to find unallocated
   space.
   This can not handle uneven space (e.g. 1G + 10G RAID0), and it should
   go through the  per-profile estimation, which handles uneven disks way
   better.

   Normally it shouldn't affect single device, but we can still over-
   estimate by taking the reserved 1M bytes as available.

- total_free_data calculation
   It's just on-disk size minus the on-disk used space, which doesn't
   take things like reserved space into consideration.

   Thus I guess the problem is mostly from this part.

Mind to give this idea a try to fix the long failing g747?

Thanks,
Qu

2026-03-25.patch_summary: This patch addresses a test failure issue in the generic/747 test, which consistently fails on btrfs due to ENOSPC errors during write/delete cycles. The problem arises from statfs overestimating available space at high fill levels, causing dd to hit ENOSPC even when there's room. To handle this, the patch breaks out of the fill loop when full, deletes a file to free space and retry on failure, and redirects dd stderr to preserve errors for debugging purposes. The test is designed to stress garbage collection through write/delete churn, not to validate space accounting.
